title: Monetary Policy Spillovers through Invoicing Currencies
abstract: This paper explores the role of trade invoicing currencies in the international spillover of monetary policy. Using high-frequency measures of Federal Reserve monetary policy shocks, I show that exchange rates, interest rates, and equity returns in countries with a larger share of dollar-invoiced imports systematically respond more to U.S. monetary policy. I document similar transmission effects from European Central Bank (ECB) monetary policy shocks to countries with euro-invoiced imports. I rationalize these findings within a New Keynesian framework. As a result of these spillovers, domestic monetary policy should be less effective in countries with traded goods invoiced in foreign currencies.

article.introduction:
The actions of the U.S.Federal Reserve Bank (FRB) are of fundamental importance for central bankers around the world. A growing literature finds that monetary policy actions of the United States significantly impact macroeconomic conditions and asset prices globally (Rey (2013), Miranda-Agrippino and Rey (2020)). What features of the global economy allow the FRB to influence global macroeconomic conditions, and to what extent do monetary policy actions from other central banks also influence outcomes abroad? Recent evidence from Gopinath (2016) shows that over 80% of traded goods are invoiced in dollars and euros, and the nominal prices of traded goods are sticky in their invoicing currency in the short run. This paper studies the role of trade invoicing currencies in transmitting monetary policy abroad.
Using high-frequency measures of FRB monetary policy shocks, I show that cross-sectional variation in the magnitude of FRB monetary policy spillovers can be explained by the use of the dollar in international trade. In response to contractionary monetary policy, countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced imports observe their currencies appreciate and their interest rates increase relative to countries with a smaller share of dollar-invoiced trade. This differential response in foreign asset prices is driven primarily by non-G10 currencies. Furthermore, I show that monetary policy spillover effects also emanate from European Central Bank (ECB) actions, which suggests that the large magnitude of U.S. monetary policy spillover effects can be explained by the dollar's dominance as a global trade currency. Building on Engel (2011), I develop an open-economy New Keynesian model that rationalizes the empirical findings. Firms in each country invoice exports in either a domestic currency or a global trade currency.1Within the model, invoicing in a global trade currency exposes a country to monetary policy spillovers from the issuer of that global trade currency, and moreover decreases the effectiveness of the domestic central bank.
I begin with the empirical analysis and document that countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced imports suffer larger spillovers from U.S. monetary policy shocks. To identify these transmission effects, I use high-frequency measures of monetary policy shocks from Acosta and Saia (2020) and measure responses in foreign exchange rates and one-year interest rates.2The monetary policy shocks capture changes in short-term interest rate instruments in tight windows around monetary policy events. The major strength of this approach is that it addresses the concern that monetary policy actions are endogenous to macroeconomics news. The identification assumption is that all public information is incorporated into asset prices prior to the start of these narrow windows around monetary policy announcements, and that any additional change in the interest rate instruments within these windows are the result of the monetary policy action.
For each country, I use trade invoicing data from Boz et al. (2020) to construct a proxy for the degree of exposure to U.S. monetary policy: its share of imports invoiced in dollars and normalized by aggregate consumption. This measure is the country's share of dollar-invoiced consumption, and captures the fraction of a country's aggregate consumption that is affected by shocks to the value of the dollar. In the theoretical section of the paper, I provide support for the view that the dollar share of consumption is the correct measure to explain exposure to U.S. monetary policy within a set of open-economy New Keynesian models with complete asset markets.
The main empirical results show that variation in the share of dollar-invoiced consumption across countries explains cross-sectional differences in monetary policy transmission. In response to a 100 basis point (bp) FRB contraction, foreign exchange rates depreciate against the dollar by an average of 971 bps. Foreign one-year interest rates on average decline by 47 bps. However, a country that experiences a one-standard-deviation increase in its share of dollar-invoiced consumption depreciates by 124 fewer bps, and its one-year interest rate increases by an additional 35 bps. These responses in foreign exchange rates and one-year interest rates are driven primarily by non-G10 currencies. Within the G10, both the exchange rate response and the interest rate response are smaller in magnitude, and the exchange rate response is not statistically significant.
The main concern with this interpretation of the results is that other underlying country-level characteristics may drive both exposures to dollar-invoiced trade and a country's reaction to U.S. monetary policy. For example, countries that import more from the United States would have a higher share of dollar-invoiced imports. These countries may also be more exposed to shocks within the United States because their domestic economies depend on the U.S.-made products themselves. This mechanism differs from the currency invoicing channel because many countries do not import heavily from the United States but are nevertheless exposed to dollar-invoiced goods. In robustness checks, I show that the share of dollar-invoiced consumption continues to explain heterogeneity in FRB spillovers even after accounting for many alternative mechanisms.
Shedding further light on the monetary policy transmission mechanism, I show that industry sectors that rely more on dollar-invoiced intermediary inputs with sticky prices respond systematically more to FRB policy. Using the World Input-Output Tables (WIOT), I estimate the share of each sector's intermediary inputs that are invoiced in dollars, and I estimate differences in the frequency of price adjustment of these imports. Sectors with a higher share of dollar-invoiced intermediary inputs with less frequent price adjustment observe lower equity returns in response to U.S. monetary contractions. These sector-level results not only strengthen the country-level results, but also provide evidence for the canonical New Keynesian transmission mechanism that relies on nominal price rigidity.
After analyzing FRB spillovers, I next use high-frequency measures of ECB monetary policy shocks from Leombroni et al. (2021) and show that countries with a higher share of euro-invoiced consumption suffer larger spillovers from ECB monetary policy. To directly compare the magnitude of FRB and ECB monetary policy spillovers, I rely tick-level euro and Euribor futures data to construct a consistent measure of monetary policy shocks across the FRB and the ECB. Spillovers from the two central banks are similar in magnitude when accounting for the share of consumption invoiced in dollars and euros. These results suggest that monetary policy transmission through invoicing currencies is a common phenomenon across central banks. Moreover, the outsized influence of the FRB may be due in part to the use of the dollar as a global trade currency.
In the theoretical section of the paper, I characterize the effect of invoicing currencies on monetary policy transmission using a New Keynesian model in which countries differ along a number of dimensions in addition to the currencies used to invoice trade. Under this model, variation in monetary policy spillovers from one central bank to a foreign country is fully captured by the share of the foreign country's consumption that is invoiced in the currency issued by the central bank. The model therefore provides a rationale for using the share of dollar- or euro-invoiced consumption to measure monetary policy exposure in the empirical section of the paper. Contractionary monetary policy from any central bank increases the relative value of its currency, which increases the price of consuming all traded goods invoiced in its currency. Countries with a larger share of consumption invoiced in the affected currency suffer a larger increase to their cost of consumption.
In response to an FRB monetary contraction, countries with a larger share of dollar-invoiced consumption decrease their consumption more, which increases their marginal utility of consumption more. This increase in the marginal utility of consumption directly translates into a real exchange rate appreciation (an increase in the real price of consumption) and an increase in real interest rates. Changes in nominal exchange rates and nominal interest rates mirror these changes in real exchange rates and real interest rates. Thus, the model rationalizes the empirical findings.
The model highlights how the use of a global trade currency to invoice exports can limit the effectiveness of monetary policy. Central banks in countries where more firms invoice their exports in foreign currency face a worse output-inflation trade-off. Because the prices of traded goods are sticky in their invoicing currency, a central bank's monetary policy affects demand for all traded goods invoiced in its own currency—domestically and abroad. When domestic firms invoice their exports in dollars, the domestic central bank loses influence over the price of its exports. The central bank's monetary policy actions, therefore, become less able to influence domestic production. Even in an environment in which exchange rates are freely floating and agents have access to sophisticated financial markets, central banks become less effective when domestic firms trade in foreign currency. Firms that invoice their exports in U.S. dollars become subject to FRB policy, regardless of where they reside.
A growing literature examines the consequences of monetary policy, using high-frequency measures of monetary policy shocks (Kuttner (2001), Gurkaynak, Sack, and Swanson (2005), Gertler and Karadi (2015), Gorodnichenko and Weber (2016), Ozdagli and Weber (2017), Nakamura and Steinsson (2018), Stavrakeva and Tang (2018)). This literature focuses primarily on the effects of U.S. shocks on U.S. outcomes. Notable exceptions include Rey (2014), Leombroni et al. (2021), Shah (2017), and Wiriadinata (2017). This paper contributes to the literature by highlighting invoicing currencies as a specific channel that explains variation in the transmission of monetary policy abroad. In this sense, this paper is most similar to Wiriadinata (2017), who shows that credit-constrained countries with more dollar-denominated debt suffer larger spillovers from U.S. monetary policy.3
The mechanism for monetary policy transmission in this paper relies on the interaction between nominal rigidities in traded goods and exchange rate movements, which is explored in the exchange rate pass-through literature.4However, this literature focuses primarily on the long-run consequences of exchange rate movements for macroeconomic variables (Gopinath and Rigobon (2008), Burstein and Gopinath (2014), Gopinath (2016)). My paper is most similar to Gopinath et al. (2020), who also use currency invoicing data from Gopinath (2016) to understand heterogeneity in exchange rate pass-through in the medium to long run. A main challenge in this literature is the fact that exchange rates are endogenous to the macroeconomic environment, and tests of exchange rate pass-through must control for all macroeconomic factors that may drive both the exchange rate and the outcome variables of interest. This paper contributes to the exchange rate pass-through literature by using exogenous monetary policy shocks to identify high-frequency responses asset prices that are a result of dollar invoicing and exchange rate movements. The empirical results highlight dollar invoicing of traded goods as a first-order determinant of shock transmission across countries.
Finally, this paper contributes to a literature in international finance that highlights different forms of country-level heterogeneity that drive persistent differences in asset returns and capital accumulation across countries (Hassan (2013), Farhi and Gabaix (2015), Hassan, Mertens, and Zhang (2016), Maggiori (2017), Richmond (2019), Hassan, Mertens, and Zhang (2020)). Differences in asset returns are attributed to the risk premia associated with investing in different countries (Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011)). Differences in the exposure to dollar-invoiced trade is a natural channel for differential exposure to U.S. monetary policy risk, which can drive differences in currency returns (Mueller, Tahbaz-Salehi, and Vedolin (2017)). As a result, systematic variation in the use of invoicing currencies in traded goods is a potential determinant of long-run differences in risk premia across countries.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the data and empirical methodology. SectionIIpresents empirical results. SectionIIIpresents the model, and SectionIVcharacterizes the equilibrium and derives theoretical predictions of the model. Finally, SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Data:
I use three sets of monetary policy shocks to study the role of invoicing currencies in monetary policy transmission for the FRB and the ECB, and to compare the magnitude of transmission across banks. Each set of monetary policy shocks measures changes in short-term interest rate instruments in narrow windows around monetary policy announcements. The validity of these shock measures relies on the assumption that all public information is incorporated into financial markets prior to the start of these windows. Hence, changes in asset prices within these windows reflect only the surprise component of monetary policy.
My main empirical results use high-frequency measures of FRB monetary policy shocks from Acosta and Saia (2020) to test for monetary policy transmission from the United States. The authors extend “policy news shocks” series of Nakamura and Steinsson (2018) to the period 2000 to 2019, which I use as my independent variable. This series captures the first principle component of changes in five different interest rate futures of various maturities in a 30-minute window around FRB policy announcements. In this sense, the series of monetary policy shocks captures changes in the federal funds rate as well as the effects of forward guidance.
I describe the two additional sets of monetary policy shocks used to capture ECB monetary policy in SectionII.Fwhen I discuss spillovers through euro-invoiced trade.
Boz et al. (2020) provide the share of a country's imports and exports invoiced in various currencies between 1990 and 2020 at an annual frequency. These data extend the currency invoicing data from Gopinath (2016) and are collected from various statistical agencies. The data capture both time-series and cross-sectional variation in currency invoicing shares in dollars, euros, and domestic currency. Gopinath (2016) provides evidence that these invoicing shares are relatively stable over time for each country.
For each countryn, I combine the currency invoicing data with macroeconomic data to generate country-level and sector-level measures that capture exposure to trade in dollars and euros. At the country level, I use the Penn World Tables to construct the share of countrynconsumption invoiced in currencyc,Mcn, to capture exposure to currencycthrough the import channel.Mcnis the product of the share of countrynimports invoiced in currencycand countryn's average imports as a share of final consumption. I also construct the share of countrynexports invoiced in currencycnormalized by GDP,Xcn, to capture exposure to currencycthrough the export channel.Xcnis the product of the share of countryn's exports invoiced in currencycwith the country's average exports as a share of GDP.
At the sector level, I use data from the WIOT to estimate the exposure of individual sectors to dollar-invoiced intermediate inputs to production as well as the frequency of price adjustment for these intermediate inputs. I describe my treatment of the sector-level data along with the analysis in SectionII.E.
For the main analysis, I focus on a set of economies for which I observe both currency invoicing and forward premia. TableIA.Xin theInternet Appendixlists the countries in my sample along with their data availability.5These countries are Australia, Canada, Cyprus, the Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Greece, Hungary, Iceland, India, Indonesia, Israel, Japan, Latvia, Lithuania, Malta, New Zealand, Norway, Poland, Saudi Arabia, Slovenia, South Korea, Sweden, Switzerland, Thailand, Turkey, the United Kingdom, and the United States. I include the Euro area as a single “country.” Across the countries in my sample, the mean dollar-invoiced share of consumption,M$n, is 0.284 and its standard deviation is 0.149. Thus, there is large variation in countries' exposures to dollar-invoiced imports.6
Overall, my sample includes monetary policy announcement days for the FRB and the ECB between January 1, 2000 and December 31, 2017. Following Nakamura and Steinsson (2018), I drop the height of the financial crisis from July 2008 to June 2009. I also drop observations at the country-date level for countries whose currencies are pegged (hard or soft currency pegs) to the central bank providing the monetary policy shock.8TableIA.Iin theInternet Appendixprovides summary statistics for monetary policy shocks and characteristics at the country and central bank levels.
article.II. Empirical Strategy and Results:
The coefficient of interest in equation (2) isγM, which captures the effect of heterogeneity in dollar invoicing on the magnitude of monetary policy transmission. A positiveγMindicates that a contractionary FRB shock increases the outcome variable more in countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced consumption. Equation (2) does not include the monetary policy shock itself because it is absorbed by the time fixed effect. Standard errors are clustered by date.
TableIestimates the effect of dollar invoicing on the transmission of FRB monetary policy to foreign exchange rates. Column (1) of TableIprovides a benchmark estimate: a point estimate of−1.24implies that if a country experiences a one-standard-deviation increase inM$n, its currency depreciates by 124 fewer bps in response to a 100 bp FRB contractionary shock. I find that currencies depreciate by an average of 971 bps in response to a 100 bp FRB contractionary shock.9Hence, the effect of currency invoicing on monetary policy transmission appears to be economically large as well as statistically significant.
The main concern with this interpretation of the results is that other economic factors drive both the magnitude of exchange rate movements and the extent of dollar invoicing in a country. Although I take the currency of invoicing to be exogenous, a large trade literature discusses many theoretical reasons why a firm should endogenously choose to invoice its exports in a particular currency.
In particular, theory suggests that a firm should choose its invoicing currency to minimize the costs of price stickiness, which can depend on the sensitivity of its marginal costs to exchange rate movements as well as the invoicing currency of its competing exporters (Burstein and Gopinath (2014), Mukhin (2018)). For example, firms that import dollar-invoiced intermediate inputs may have marginal costs that are stable in U.S. dollars and should therefore invoice their exports in dollars. Alternatively, Gopinath and Stein (2018) provide a model in which financing is more available in dollars, and hence, firms want to invoice in dollars to service dollar-denominated liabilities.
Unfortunately, there is no consensus on what factors determine the observed patterns in currency choice across countries. There is evidence that U.S. dollar invoicing is more prevalent among more homogeneous goods (e.g., mineral products) while invoicing in the domestic currency is more prevalent among differentiated product sectors such as footwear (Goldberg and Tille (2008)). However, there is no simple empirical relationship between industry structure, the sensitivity of marginal costs to exchange rate risk, macroeconomic risks, and invoicing currency choice (Gopinath (2016)).10As a result, I follow common practice in the exchange rate pass-through literature and take the invoicing of imported goods at the country level to be exogenous.
In this paper, I control for a number of additional variables that may drive dollar invoicing at the country level. Nevertheless, I acknowledge there may still exist an alternative channel that drives both currency choice and variation in responses to U.S. monetary policy.
Columns (2) to (6) in TableIshow that the explanatory power of the dollar-invoiced share of consumption is robust to controls for country size, the distance between countries, the share of each country's imports from the United States, and a country's dollar-denominated net debt position. Taken alone, each variable significantly affects the magnitude of monetary policy spillovers from the United States. For example, countries that are farther from the United States depreciate more against the dollar in response to U.S. monetary contractions.11However, when I control for both distance and currency invoicing, only the currency invoicing variable significantly explains cross-sectional variation in U.S. monetary policy transmission. The same is true for each country's share of trade with the United States and each country's dollar-denominated net debt asset position. Nevertheless, many of these potentially confounding factors are correlated with country size. I therefore control for country size in each of the regressions hereafter.
TableIIdocuments the impact of dollar invoicing on FRB monetary policy spillovers in foreign one-year interest rates. Column (1) provides a benchmark point estimate of 0.35. If a country experiences a one-standard-deviation increase inM$n, its nominal interest rate will increase by an additional 35 bps in response to a 100 bp FRB contractionary shock. Overall, I find that foreign one-year interest rates decrease by an average of 47 bps in response to a 100 bp FRB contractionary shock. Thus, the cross-sectional effect of currency invoicing on monetary policy transmission appears to be economically large as well as statistically significant. Columns (2) to (6) show that variation inM$nsignificantly explains cross-sectional variation in U.S. monetary policy transmission even after controlling for country size, the distance between countries, the importance of U.S. imports, and dollar-denominated net foreign asset positions.12
Finally, TableIIIestimates equation (2) separately for the subsample of G10 and non-G10 currencies.13The first column shows that the impact of dollar invoicing on U.S. monetary policy transmission to foreign exchange rates is not statistically significant for the G10 currencies, although, the coefficient continues to be negative. In contrast, column (3) suggests that the results in TableIare driven largely by the response of the non-G10 currencies to U.S. monetary policy. Columns (2) and (4) of TableIIIshow that the differential interest rate response is significant in both the G10 and non-G10 subsamples. However, the magnitude of the effect of dollar invoicing is also larger in the subsample of non-G10 currencies.
I interpret movements in one-year interest rates as an outcome of financial markets. However, an additional possibility is that the results in TableIIare due to foreign central banks systematically responding to FRB policy decisions, or a result of financial market participants' expectations of foreign central bank responses. Specifically, central banks in countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced consumption may systematically increase and decrease their policy rates more in response to changes in U.S. monetary policy.
To address these concerns, I obtain central bank policy rates for the countries in my sample from the Bank of International Settlements.14TableIVestimates equation (2) using the changes in a foreign central bank's policy rate around an FRB monetary policy shock as the dependent variable. Column (1) measures the change in policy rates from the end of the previous month to the end of the current month of the monetary policy shock. In response to a 100 bp FRB monetary policy shock, countries with a one-standard-deviation higherM$nobserve a 2.3 bp decline in their own central bank policy rate in the month of the FRB monetary policy shock. This point estimate is statistically insignificant. Column (2) measures the change in policy rate through the end of the month following a monetary policy shock. A country with a one-standard-deviation higherM$nobserves a 0.151 pp decline in its own central bank policy rate in the two-month window surrounding a 100 bp FRB monetary policy shock. This result is also not statistically significant. I therefore find no evidence that countries with higher shares of dollar-invoiced consumption respond systematically more to U.S. monetary policy actions, and that the empirical results are driven by the systematic responses of foreign central banks.
In sum, the results in TablesIandIIprovide strong empirical evidence that countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced consumption suffer larger spillover effects from FRB monetary policy. FRB monetary policy contractions act like contractionary shocks in foreign countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced consumption.
TableVshows that variation in export currency invoicing does not explain variation in the responses of exchange rates and interest rates to FRB policy shocks. Columns (1) and (3) show that variation inX$ndoes not explain the variation in the dependent variables by itself. In contrast, columns (2) and (4) show that the invoicing currency of imports(M$n)continues to explain variation in FRB spillovers after controlling for the invoicing currency of exports. Therefore, I focus on testing the role of the invoicing currency of imports in the remainder of this paper.15
The inability of the invoicing currency of exports to explain movements in interest rates and exchange rates is surprising given strong empirical results linking import currency invoicing to monetary policy transmission. Intuitively, we might expect firms that export in dollars to suffer a negative demand shock when the dollar appreciates relative to other currencies. Fluctuations in the dollar should therefore greatly impact the wealth of firms and households in countries that export in dollars. However, the model in SectionIIIshows that firms and households can insure against these wealth shocks in economic environments with sufficient risk-sharing. The theoretical section of the paper provides intuition for this asymmetry between the explanatory power of the invoicing currencies of imports and the invoicing currencies of exports.
In this section, I describe a number of additional robustness checks for the main empirical results. The tables containing these results have all been relegated to theInternet Appendix. Overall, dollar invoicing continues to explain cross-sectional heterogeneity in the responses of foreign exchange rates and interest rates to U.S. monetary policy shocks.
One concern is that the empirical results are driven by the global financial crisis. TableIA.IVshows the results of estimating equation (2) are similar when only using observations of monetary policy shocks prior to the height of the Great Recession (July 2008).
TableIA.Vshows the results are similar when using alternative measures of monetary policy shocks. One concern is the Nakamura and Steinsson (2018) (and thus Acosta and Saia (2020)) shocks are not pure monetary policy shocks, but instead contain information about future macroeconomic outcomes. Column (1) of TableIA.Vshows the results are similar when using shocks based on changes in three-month euro futures alone, which should contain less information about future macroeconomic outcomes. In column (2), I drop monetary policy shocks smaller than 5 bps and show my results are not driven by small shocks. In fact, the empirical results become more pronounced in the sample of large shocks. Ozdagli and Weber (2017) show financial markets react differently to expansionary versus contractionary monetary policy shocks. In column (3), I test this hypothesis. The exchange rate results are driven more by surprise monetary policy contractions, whereas interest rates do not appear to respond differently to positive or negative shocks.
TableIA.VIshows the results are similar when using different measures of dollar invoicing exposure. In column (1), I control for another measure of trade exposure, namely, a country's imports to and exports from the United States, as well as the country's relative size to the United States. In column (2), I normalizeM$nby output rather than consumption.
TableIA.VIIand theInternet AppendixSectionI.Cshow the main results are robust to using alternative specifications of standard errors. TableIA.VIIshows the main results remain significant when using Driscoll-Kray standard errors to allow for cross-sectional and time-series correlation in error terms. In fact, the Driscoll-Kray standard errors tend to be tighter, which is likely due to negative time-series correlation in the error term. Hence, I continue to use the more conservative standard errors and cluster by date. TheInternet AppendixSectionI.Cestimates equation (2), and shows that an estimator based on identification by heteroskedasticity delivers similar results as OLS.
Finally, TableIA.VIIIshows the dollar-invoiced share of consumption is better at explaining cross-sectional variation in spillovers in the short run. I estimate equation (2) using interest rates of different maturities. The effect of dollar invoicing decreases with maturity and becomes statistically insignificant.
In this section, I provide sector-level analysis of spillovers from U.S. monetary policy. Sector-level analysis provides additional evidence of the importance of invoicing currencies in monetary policy transmission as well as evidence for the New Keynesian transmission mechanism through sticky prices. In particular, sector-level analysis allows me to control for an important factor that may impact monetary policy transmission: price stickiness in traded goods.
I conduct my analysis at the GICS sector level. The outcome variables of interest are the GICS sector-level equity index returns, which are available through Bloomberg. GICS divides the economy into 11 sectors. I exclude the Financials, Healthcare, Real Estate, Utilities, and Communication Services sectors because these sectors do not have good coverage in the cross section of countries. This leaves me with the following six sectors in my analysis: Consumer Durables, Consumer Staples, Energy, Information Technology, Industrials, and Materials.
The standard monetary policy transmission mechanism in open-economy New Keynesian models relies on the price rigidity of traded goods (Clarida, Gali, and Gertler (2002)). Thus far, I have ignored heterogeneity in price rigidity at the country level. However, the price rigidity of traded goods can vary significantly across sectors of the economy (Burstein and Gopinath (2014)). For example, commodities such as crude oil are invoiced in dollars but their prices change frequently, while the prices of other products, such as electronics, tend to be more sticky.
For each sectorjin countrynin my sample, I estimate its exposure to dollar invoicing through the share of its dollar-invoiced (imported) intermediary inputs as a fraction of total intermediary inputs, and the frequency of price adjustment of these intermediary inputs. First, I use sector-level trade data from the WIOT to calculate the value of imported inputs as a fraction of total intermediate inputs for each country-sector. I also estimate the value of exports as a fraction of total sector output for each country-sector. These two measures capture the importance of traded goods for each country-sector.
Next, I estimate the price rigidity of imports across different sectors. A large trade literature classifies traded goods based on the method by which prices are set (Rauch (1999)). These classifications are: goods traded on an organized exchange, reference priced goods, and differentiated goods. Gopinath and Rigobon (2008) shows the frequency of price adjustment varies greatly across these groups. A total of 87% of imports traded on an organized exchange adjust their prices every month, whereas only 30% of reference priced imports and 7% of differentiated imports adjust their prices every month.16For each sector, I use the Rauch classification of traded goods and the WIOT to calculate the share of goods in each sector that are not traded on an organized exchange.17I consider the goods not traded on an organized exchange to have rigid prices.
For each sectorjin countryn, I constructθM,$,jnas the product of the share of dollar-invoiced imports in countryn, the sector's imports as a fraction of intermediate inputs, and the fraction of imports with rigid prices.18Similarly, I also construct the termθX,$,jnas the product of the share of dollar-invoiced exports in countryn, the sector's exports as a fraction of output, and the fraction of exports with rigid prices.
TableIA.IIsummarizes the average exposure of each sector to dollar invoicing and also lists the number of countries for which I observe equity returns. Consistent with the previous analysis, I include the Euro area as a single country, and I use the value-weighted return of all Euro area countries. On average, the energy sector has the greatest import exposure because the vast majority of intermediate inputs into the energy sector are imported.
TableVIshows sectors relying more on dollar-invoiced intermediary inputs with rigid prices observe lower returns in response to a surprise FRB contraction. Column (1) shows in response to a 100 bp U.S. monetary contraction, sectors with a one-standard-deviation higherθM,$,jnwill observe a 265 bp lower return. This effect remains consistent in magnitude and significance across specifications. Column (3) controls for additional country and industry level variables. In particular, I control for the share of imports from the United States to each sectorj.
Column (2) of TableVIsplitsθM,$,jninto two main components.Rigid Import Shareis the average faction of goods with fixed prices in each month, which captures price rigidity in sectorjof countryn.USD Import Sharecaptures the share of dollar-invoiced imports interacted with the share of imported inputs into sectorjof countryn. The estimates in column (2) suggest both increases price rigidity and more dollar-invoiced inputs are important for explaining cross-sectional variation spillovers at the industry level.
Overall, TableVIprovides evidence consistent with the main empirical results in SectionII.A. Sectors that rely more on dollar-invoiced intermediary inputs are more exposed to U.S. monetary policy spillovers. Moreover, these results provide evidence for the standard New Keynesian transmission mechanism, which relies on price rigidities.19
In this section, I provide evidence that the invoicing currency of imports explains the magnitude of ECB monetary policy spillovers as well. In contrast to the U.S. dollar, a smaller fraction of traded goods is invoiced in euros outside of the European Monetary Union. Moreover, a number of other large macroeconomic events occurred in Europe during the sample period (e.g., the European debt crisis). Nevertheless, the results in this section are important because they suggest the international transmission of monetary policy through invoicing currencies is not unique to the Federal Reserve.
I study the responses of interest rates and exchange rates to two additional sets of monetary policy shocks. The first comes from Leombroni et al. (2021), who measure changes in interest rate swaps around ECB policy announcements. The authors construct an “interest rate shock” that capture the effect of ECB monetary policy on the future path of interest rates using high-frequency movements in interest rate swaps around ECB announcements.
The ECB shocks from Leombroni et al. (2021) and the FRB shocks from Acosta and Saia (2020) are constructed differently and rely on different underlying assets, which makes a direct comparison of their effects difficult.20To shed light on differences in monetary policy transmission across central banks, I construct a data set of monetary policy shocks consistently for both the FRB and the ECB. Specifically, using tick-level data provided by Tick Data, I measure changes in three-month interest rate futures denominated in dollars (three-month Libor) and euros (three-month Euribor) around both FRB and ECB monetary policy announcements. For the ECB monetary policy shock, I measure changes in a window that captures both the target rate announcement and the following press conference.21
The results in TableVIIshow countries with more euro-invoiced consumption are more exposed to ECB monetary policy shocks. Columns (1) and (2) show that variation in the euro-invoiced share of consumption explains heterogeneity in monetary policy transmission from the ECB. Just like the earlier results with dollar invoicing, countries with more euro-invoiced consumption saw their exchange rates depreciate less, and their interest rates increase more in response to ECB monetary contractions. Columns (3) and (4) show these results are robust to limiting the estimation to ECB monetary policy announcements prior to the Great Recession. Finally, TableVIIIshows these euro invoicing continues to explain variation in monetary policy spillovers within the subsample of G10 currencies.
TableIXestimates equation (5) using the pooled sample of monetary policy shocks from both central banks. In general, FRB monetary policy spillovers through invoicing currencies do not appear uniquely large. The estimate of the coefficient on the triple-interaction term is not significant in any specification.
The results in this section suggest policy spillovers through invoicing currencies are a more general phenomenon. The use of the U.S. dollar as the dominant invoicing currency can help explain the outsized influence of the FRB in the world. In a world in which the U.S. dollar is no longer the global trade currency, a new central bank would gain influence over the global economy. For example, Hiro and Masahiro (2016) provide evidence that between 1970 and 1990, the German mark and the U.K. pound were more widely used as regional trade invoicing currencies in Europe. Hence, countries may have historically suffered monetary policy spillovers from the Bundesbank or Bank of England. Looking forward, the results suggest the FRB may lose global influence as countries such as China and Russia attempt to trade more in domestic currencies.22
article.III. The Model:
In this section, I show the canonical open-economy New Keynesian model can account for the empirical results in SectionII. The model builds on canonical open-economy New Keynesian models (Floden and Wilander (2006), Engel (2011)) by allowing for heterogeneity in currency invoicing across countries. In each country, an exogenous fraction of firms invoice their exports in a domestic currency, whereas the remainder of firms invoice their exports in a global trade currency. I also allow for differences in country size and in household preferences for varieties of traded goods to explore other potential channels for monetary policy spillovers. However, an important outcome of the model is that all heterogeneity in spillover effects is captured by differences in the currency invoicing of imports, and differences in country size have no direct impact on the variation in monetary policy spillovers.
It is well known that the canonical open-economy model with complete asset markets exhibits a number of empirical shortcomings. In particular, the canonical model predicts a tight link between aggregate consumption growth and exchange rates, which is counterfactual to the data (Backus and Smith (1993)). The benchmark model in this paper fails along this dimension as well. As a result, I also provide a segmented-markets extension in which only a subset of households within each country have access to complete asset markets. The segmented markets extension reinforces the intuition from the benchmark complete markets case, and shows the logic behind the complete markets model extends into environments where aggregate consumption growth and asset prices are disconnected.
Two time periods exist:t=1,2. A unit mass of households is partitioned into three countries. For expositional purposes, I label these countries the United States (US), Japan (JP), and Europe (EU). Letμndenote the mass of households residing in countryn. Households in each country gain utility from consumption and disutility from providing labor services. In countryn, a massμnof firms also exists, and each firm produces a unique intermediate traded good. Households supply labor to all firms within their own country in the production of intermediate traded goods, and share in the profits of all sales of intermediate traded goods. In this manner, all households within a country are identical.
At the start of each period, each household in countrynis endowed withYN,tnunits of the nontraded good.
The following pricing assumption captures the key feature of the international price system described in Gopinath (2016): nominal prices of traded goods are fixed in their currency of invoicing in the short run. In the model, firms invoice their exports (sales to foreign households) in either domestic currency or U.S. dollars. In countrym, a fractionχ$mof firms invoice their exports in dollars. The remaining fraction1−χ$mof firms invoice their exports in the domestic currency. Domestic sales of intermediate traded goods are invoiced in the domestic currency.
I view the second period as the long run and assume nominal prices adjust freely in the second period. Thus, monetary policy actions only affect real allocations in the first period.
Intuitively, one can think of an increase in the aggregate nominal price level as a currency devaluation. When the central bank increasesP∼tn, more units of countryncurrency are required to purchase a unit of final consumption in countryn. Hence, each unit of countryncurrency purchases fewer real consumption goods. This form of monetary policy provides a convenient modeling shorthand for other forms of monetary policy. For example, it is equivalent to introducing money into the model, forcing households to purchase goods using cash holdings, and allowing the central bank to control the money supply.
Households trade a complete set of state-contingent claims denominated in U.S. dollars. Even though households can insure against the monetary policy actions of all central banks, monetary policy still affects the allocation of traded goods by changing the relative prices traded and nontraded goods invoiced in different currencies. The assumption of complete asset markets simply allows households to freely shift their wealth across states of the world.
Qtis the price of a state-contingent claim (in dollars). I multiplyQtby the nominal exchange rateE∼tUS,nto translate the domestic currency value of household wealth into dollars.κnis a transfer across households, which decentralizes the Social Planner's problem with unit Pareto weights.24
The market clearing condition for intermediate traded goodjproduced in countrymisYT,m,j,t=μmNm,j,t=∑n∈{US,JP,EU}μnCT,m,j,tn. Households consume their endowment of nontraded goods,YN,tn=CN,tn. The labor market clearing condition for households in countrymis,Ntm=∫0μnNm,j,tdj.
An equilibrium in this economy is a set of consumption and labor supply allocations for each household{CT,m,j,tn,CN,tn,Ntn}, a set of intermediate output, labor demand, and nominal prices for each intermediate firm{YT,m,j,tn,Nm,j,t,P∼T,m,j,tn}, a set of traded good prices and nontraded good prices{P∼T,tn,P∼N,tn}, a set of nominal wages for each country and a nominal exchange rate{W∼tn,E∼tn,m}, such that households maximize utility subject to their budget constraints, and the resource constraints are satisfied.25I log-linearize the model around the deterministic solution.26Lower case variables denote logs.
article.IV. Theoretical Results:
I focus on first period prices and allocations because nominal rigidities only affect allocations in the short run. Accordingly, I drop the timetsubscript whenever possible. All variables represent first period prices and allocations unless otherwise noted.
Crucially, differences in country size,μn, have no direct impact on the effects of U.S. monetary policy. International monetary policy transmission occurs through the demand for traded goods, which is fully captured by the parametersαmn. In reality, we may expect countries to import more traded goods from larger countries. However, this explicit link between country size and consumption shares is not modeled within this paper. As a result, the country size parameters drop completely out of all expressions of monetary policy transmission. In other words, under this model, country size affects the magnitude of international monetary transmission only through its potential effect on the trade parametersαmn.
M$JPis the theoretical counterpart to the dollar share of consumption used in the empirical section of the paper to measure a country's exposure to FRB policy. Within this model,M$nis a sufficient statistic that fully captures the effect of cross-country heterogeneity on U.S. monetary policy transmission abroad. Countries with a larger share of dollar-invoiced consumption suffer larger spillovers from the FRB. Households that consume more dollar-invoiced intermediate traded goods observe a price increase over a larger share of their consumption bundle. They shift away from consuming traded goods, and their aggregate consumption falls more.
Consistent with SectionII, the share of each country's exports invoiced in dollars does not directly affect the magnitude of U.S. monetary policy spillovers once I control forM$nbecause households trade state-contingent claims to insure against changes in income. Changes in the value of the dollar affect firm revenues in each state of the world. However, households smooth out the volatility in their income from intermediate traded goods firms by buying and selling state contingent claims. As a result, only the aggregate wealth across all states of the world matters for determining consumption.
The fact that the currency denomination of exports does not directly affect aggregate consumption highlights the role of financial market completeness in determining the effects of monetary policy. Agents in this economy can fully insure against wealth shocks that arise from changes in exchange rates in an environment with well-functioning financial markets. However, agents will still want to shift their consumption of goods away from states of the world in which the price of consumption is high, and toward states of the world in which the price of consumption is low. As a result, the share of dollar-invoiced consumption remains important for determining spillover effects.
Equation (11) shows countries with a higherM$nappreciate with respect to countries with a lower share of dollar-invoiced consumption. Contractionary U.S. monetary policy decreases aggregate consumption more in foreign countries with a larger share of dollar-invoiced consumption. Hence, the marginal utility of consumption increases relative to countries with a smaller share of dollar-invoiced consumption, which leads to an appreciation of the real price level and real exchange rate. Within this model, the real exchange rate appreciation directly translates into nominal exchange rate appreciation.
Contractionary U.S. policy increases the foreign marginal utility of consumption in the short run,C1n, which increases both real and nominal interest rates in the foreign country. Equation (10) shows all heterogeneity in the transmission of U.S. policy to foreign consumption is captured byM$n. Hence, all heterogeneity in the transmission of U.S. policy to foreign interest rates is also captured byM$n. Interest rates will increase more in countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced consumption because households in these countries consume less and feel poorer in the short run.
Prediction 1.Holding foreign monetary policy constant, U.S. contractionary policy should:


The empirical results in SectionIIare consistent with both parts of Prediction1. In response to contractionary shocks from the FRB, countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced consumption(M$n)saw their exchange rates appreciate and their interest rates increase relative to countries with a lowerM$n. Although the asset pricing predictions of the model are consistent with the empirical results, aggregate consumption is unlikely to react instantaneously on any given day and is unlikely to drive high-frequency changes in asset prices. Instead, these theoretical predictions provide justification for why contractionary U.S. policy generates short-run contractionary effects in countries with more dollar-invoiced consumption. The empirical results suggest participants in financial markets expect short-run spillover effects from FRB policy shocks and incorporate these effects into asset prices.
Within the model, the magnitude of monetary policy spillover effects from any central bank is summarized by the shares of consumption invoiced in that central bank's currency.
Prediction 2.Controlling for the share of consumption that is invoiced in each country's currency, the magnitude of spillover effects from Japanese monetary policy and the European monetary policy are the same as the magnitude of the spillover effects from U.S monetary policy.

For each increase in a country's share of consumption invoiced in yen (M¥n), the country's currency appreciates by an additionalγΓunits against the yen. This effect is exactly the same as that of dollar invoicing on U.S. monetary policy transmission. I derive results showing the symmetry in monetary policy transmission to consumption and interest rates in theInternet AppendixSectionII.F.
This symmetry result provides a theoretical rationale for the large spillover effects from U.S. monetary policy, as well as intuition for the results in TableIX. In both the empirical exercise and the theoretical model, the FRB has an outsized influence over the global economy because of the widespread use of the dollar as an invoicing currency. The model suggests if the world were to move to a different equilibrium where the euro or yen was predominantly used to invoice traded goods, then the United States would lose much of its ability to influence the global economy.
Both the empirical results and the theoretical model highlight the role of invoicing currencies in the implementation of monetary policy. The trade-off between output and inflation is central to the implementation of monetary policy in the New Keynesian framework. The use of the dollar as a global invoicing currency affects the effectiveness of domestic monetary policy in the rest of the world.
Proposition 1.Holding foreign monetary policy constant, the response of domestic traded production to domestic monetary policy is decreasing in the share of the country's dollar-invoiced exports(χ$n). Hence, central banks of countries in which a larger share of firms invoice their exports in a foreign currency face a steeper Phillips Curve.


Proof.See theInternet AppendixSectionII.E.■

A country's central bank policy affects both domestic and foreign demand for its products. Suppose the central bank of Japan engages in contractionary policy. Part of the contractionary effect comes from households decreasing consumption of Japanese goods in the rest of the world because contractionary policy increases the relative cost of yen invoiced traded goods. This effect of Japanese monetary policy on foreign consumption of Japanese goods is directly proportional to the share of Japanese firms that invoice their exports in yen. In the limiting case, if no firms in Japan invoice in yen, then changes to the relative value of the yen have no impact on the relative price of Japanese traded goods abroad. In this case, Japanese monetary policy would only affect domestic demand. Hence, the effect of Japanese monetary policy on its domestic output, as well as other macroeconomic outcomes, falls as more firms invoice in U.S. dollars.
Given that many countries invoice their exports in U.S. dollars or euros, Proposition1implies the central banks of these countries incur greater inflationary costs when trying to manage their domestic economies. These effects exist even in an environment with complete asset markets and freely floating exchange rates. Thus, central banks in countries in which a large fraction of firms invoice their exports in foreign currency are less effective at implementing their own monetary policy.
Finally, theInternet AppendixSectionII.Gextends the model in SectionIIIand characterizes the effect of currency invoicing on monetary policy transmission when markets are segmented. Although the complete asset markets model is an important benchmark, it has some well-known empirical shortcomings. For example, most households in the United States and in other developed countries do not hold sophisticated financial instruments or international assets (Giannetti and Koskinen (2010)). As a result, many authors argue for a segmented markets model that breaks the tight link between consumption and asset prices (Alvarez, Atkeson, and Kehoe (2002)). I show the intuition derived in SectionIIIcontinues to hold in a model with a more realistic asset market structure.
Moreover, incomplete asset markets breaks the tight link between changes in aggregate consumption and movements in asset prices across countries. In response to U.S. monetary contractions, inactive households and active households respond differently across countries. This is because active households can partially hedge their traded consumption risk, whereas inactive households cannot. As a result, changes in the consumption of active households and asset prices are disconnected from changes in aggregate consumption.
article.V. Conclusion:
This paper documents the role of invoicing currencies in international monetary policy transmission. Countries with a higher share of dollar-invoiced consumption suffer larger spillovers from U.S. monetary policy shocks. Moreover, both empirical and theoretical evidence suggest the widespread use of the dollar in international trade can help explain the outsized role of the FRB in influencing the global economy. In the theory section of the paper, I provide support for each of the empirical findings in a benchmark open-economy New Keynesian model that captures key characteristics of the international price system: the vast majority of imports and exports in the world are invoiced in very few currencies, and goods prices tend to be sticky in their currency of invoicing in the short run. As a result of these spillover effects, I show the central banks of countries in which firms invoice their exports in a foreign currency will be less effective at implementing domestic policy.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
